chosen_title: The ₹2 lakh cash limit most people don't know — Section 269ST
caption:
Most people think cash is 'private money' — Section 269ST quietly disagrees.

Under Section 269ST of the Income Tax Act, receiving ₹2 lakh or more in cash from ONE person — in a single day, a single transaction, or for a single event — can attract a penalty equal to 100% of the amount received. It falls on YOU, the receiver. Not the person who handed it over.

Illustration: wedding guests hand you ₹3 lakh cash collectively from one family. Even if it's a fully legal gift, you could face a ₹3 lakh penalty notice. (Illustrative only — actual liability depends on facts, amounts per person, and applicable exemptions.)

This trap quietly hits people at weddings, property token payments, contractor or doctor fees, and informal 'family loans'. The giver walks away; the receiver holds the notice.

What to do: keep any single cash receipt under ₹2 lakh per person per day — for anything bigger, insist on a bank transfer. It's instant, free, and leaves zero penalty risk.

Already made a large cash transaction? Chat with us — we'll tell you exactly where you stand.

Note: general information, not professional advice — talk to us for your specific case.

Source: Section 269ST, Income Tax Act, 1961

slides.myth: Cash is private — no one tracks how much you receive.
slides.truth: Section 269ST penalises the RECEIVER 100% of any cash above ₹2 lakh.

slides.headline: Wedding. Property deal. Family loan. Section 269ST doesn't ask why.

slides.label: THE NUMBERS
